title: Quiz: Strong Subadditivity
description: Test your understanding of strong subadditivity

questions.question: Strong subadditivity states that:
questions.explanation: SSA is S(ABC) + S(B) ≤ S(AB) + S(BC). Option (c) is subadditivity. Option (d) is false for quantum states.

questions.question: The conditional mutual information I(A:C|B) equals:
questions.explanation: I(A:C|B) = S(AB) + S(BC) - S(ABC) - S(B) = S(A|B) - S(A|BC). SSA says this is non-negative.

questions.question: Strong subadditivity implies that S(A|BC) ≤ S(A|B).
questions.explanation: True. SSA can be rewritten as S(A|BC) ≤ S(A|B), meaning conditioning on more information never increases entropy.

questions.question: SSA saturates (equality holds) when:

options.text: The state is a product state
The state is a quantum Markov chain

questions.explanation: SSA saturates for quantum Markov chains: states where C can be recovered from B alone. This includes (but is not limited to) product states.

questions.question: Which inequality is NOT a consequence of SSA?
questions.explanation: Concavity of entropy is an independent property, not derived from SSA. Araki-Lieb, subadditivity, and monotonicity of MI all follow from SSA.

questions.question: Conditional mutual information I(A:C|B) can be negative in quantum mechanics.
questions.explanation: False. SSA guarantees I(A:C|B) ≥ 0. Unlike conditional entropy, conditional mutual information is always non-negative.

questions.question: In holography (AdS/CFT), SSA constrains:

options.text: The temperature of the black hole

questions.explanation: The Ryu-Takayanagi formula relates boundary entropy to bulk minimal surface areas. SSA then constrains the possible geometries.

questions.question: Which are equivalent formulations of SSA? (Select all)
questions.explanation: a, b, c are all equivalent forms of SSA. d is false (would be superadditivity, which doesn't hold).
